steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.content: The phrase "fellow OECD member nation Finland" uses the country name as a restrictive appositive — it specifies which nation is meant, so no punctuation should separate "nation" from "Finland."

steps.title: Match to choices
steps.content:
The rule: a restrictive appositive that identifies which one must not be set off by any punctuation.
  • Choice A — "nation—Finland.": a dash sets off parenthetical or supplementary information — the country name here is essential (restrictive), not supplementary. ✗
  • Choice B — "nation: Finland.": a colon introduces a list or explanation after an independent clause — "in fellow OECD member nation" is not an independent clause, so a colon is incorrect. ✗
  • Choice C — "nation, Finland.": a comma signals a non-restrictive appositive, implying "nation" already identifies a unique referent — but here the name is needed to specify which member. ✗
  • Choice D — "nation Finland.": no punctuation between "nation" and "Finland" — the name sits directly against the noun it identifies. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
